text:
RPD File No. / N° de dossier de la SPR : TA8-08432
TA8-08452
TA8-19583
Private Proceeding / Huis clos
Reasons and Decision ? Motifs et décision
Claimant(s)
XXXX XXXX XX
XXXXXX XXXX XXX
XXXXXX XXXXX
(a.k.a. XXXX XXXX)
Demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Date(s) of Hearing
August 31, 2012
September 17, 2012
Date(s) de l'audience
Place of Hearing
Toronto, Ontario
Lieu de l'audience
Date of Decision
October 26, 2012
Date de la décision
Panel
C. Buttigieg
Tribunal
Counsel for the Claimant(s)
Philip Allchin
Conseil(s) du (de la/des) demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Tribunal Officer
N/A
Agent(e) du tribunal
Designated Representative(s)
XXXX XXXX
Représentant(e)(s) désigné(e)(s)
Counsel for the Minister
N/A
Conseil du (de la) ministre
REASONS FOR DECISION
[1] XXXX XXXX, (the principal claimant hereinafter referred to as "the claimant") is a citizen of Georgia. Her husband XXXX XXXX ("the male claimant"), and their minor daughter, XXXX XXXX ("the minor claimant"), are citizens of Greece. They claim refugee protection pursuant to section 96 and subsection 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA).
[2] The claimant was appointed the Designated Representative for the minor claimant.
ALLEGATIONS
[3] The details of the claimants' allegations are documented in their Personal Information Form (PIF) narratives, PIF amendments and testimony. The claimant met a man by the name of XXXX in 2001. XXXX showed an interest in the claimant and wanted him to marry her. She turned down his advances. XXXX and his friends tried to kidnap her in 2003 by dragging her into their car, but the claimant managed to escape. She sought medical attention and tried to get help from the police, but she was unsuccessful.
[4] In XXXX 2004, the claimant and her friend XXXX were traveling in a taxi. XXXX asked the taxi to stop at a nearby store so she could run an errand. Once XXXX exited the taxi, XXXX and two of his friends entered the taxi and drove off. She was held by XXXX and his friends for a few days before being released. She was sexually assaulted during this time.
[5] She tried to get police protection, but no help was forthcoming. She remained at home. She knew that after having been kidnapped, she would be undesirable for marriage to anyone, according to custom.
[6] The claimant met the male claimant on XXXX XXXX XXXX, 2005. The male claimant is a citizen of Greece and traveled back and forth between Greece and Georgia. They got married in XXXX 2006. The claimant moved to Greece in XXXX 2006 where she enjoyed temporary residence in Greece. The minor claimant was born in XXXX 2009.
[7] On XXXX XXXX 2008, XXXX telephoned the claimant at her home in Greece. She agreed to meet him at a nearby restaurant. XXXX threatened to harm her and her family if she did not agree to stay with him. The claimant managed to convince XXXX to give her two weeks to get her affairs in order.
[8] The claimant never sought refugee protection or police protection in Greece.
[9] The male claimant worked for a XXXX XXXX in Greece. One of the XXXX buses operated by this company was involved in an accident. The claimant helped to find a replacement bus no windows at the back, as per his employer's request. It later turned out that this company was involved in human smuggling and many people were arrested. The male claimant managed to avoid the authorities, but he was blamed by the mafia running this smuggling ring, including a man named XXXX, for causing such problems. The mafia started to pursue the male claimant.
[10] The male claimant never sought police protection in Greece.
[11] Fearing harm at the hands of the mafia and XXXX, the male claimant left Greece and traveled to Canada on XXXX XXXX 2008. He made a claim for refugee protection on 08 October 2008, alleging that he was a citizen of Georgia and no other country. He subsequently changed his allegation, saying that he was only a citizen of Greece.
[12] The claimant and the minor claimant traveled to Canada on XXXX XXXX 2008. She no longer enjoyed permanent residence, which was based on her marriage to the male claimant. She and the minor claimant made claims for refugee protection on 01 May 2008.
BACKGROUND
[13] As per the claimants' request, the hearing was conducted with the assistance of a Russian-speaking interpreter.
[14] The Chairperson's Gender Guidelines1 were taken into account when considering the process of the hearing and the facts in this case. All relevant factors, such as the social and cultural context in which the claimant found herself, were guided with consideration of the Chairperson's Gender Guidelines. Given the sensitive nature of the allegations, the panel is cognizant of the difficulties faced by the claimant in establishing her claim including cultural factors, the milieu of the hearing room, and the stress inherent in responding to oral questions through an interpreter. The panel has taken these considerations into account in arriving at negative credibility findings. The panel addressed the claimant with heightened sensitivity and avoided unnecessary detail when asking questions.
[15] A first sitting was scheduled for 31 August 2012 at 8:30am. The hearing could not proceed as the claimant's babysitter failed to turn up to take care of their three minor children. In an effort to accommodate the claimant, the panel and the parties waited until 11:00am before agreeing to postpone the hearing.
[16] A second sitting was held on 17 September 2012. Counsel had requested that the claimant testify first and without the presence of the other claimants. The other claimants agreed with this arrangement, although the panel reminded the claimants of their right to be present throughout the hearing. Nonetheless, they agreed that they would not be present for that part of the hearing when the claimant testified.
[17] These claims were joined with that of the male claimant's parents and brother. They were subsequently disjoined in light of sensitive allegations that only the claimant and her husband are aware of.
[18] The panel offered the claimant multiple breaks. She requested that her husband not be questioned too much on what happened to her. Taking into account the Gender Guidelines and the claimant's wishes, the panel did not ask the male claimant any questions pertaining to her allegations of sexual assault.
[19] The claimant testified that XXXX sexually assaulted her. The claimant does not speak about this incident in her PIF narrative. In fact, she says that XXXX agreed not to touch her. In light of the Chairperson's Gender Guidelines, the panel draws no negative inference with respect to the discrepancy in this regard. Indeed, the panel appreciates that it may be difficult to speak and/or write about such incidents. The panel further notes that not only does this discrepancy concern an omission, but the claimant explicitly wrote that XXXX agreed not to touch her. Nonetheless, the panel draws no negative inference with respect to her credibility.
Medical Reports
[20] Counsel disclosed letters pertaining to the claimant from medical doctors2 and a counselor.3 Neither the credentials of the medical doctors nor the counsellor were provided. The panel notes that these letters are not psychological/psychiatric assessments. These letters also do not provide what these therapy or counselling sessions consisted of, the duration of these sessions and/or what form of psychological assessment, if any, was conducted.
[21] The panel notes that, with respect to one of the letters from a doctor at a medical clinic in Toronto, there are several basic grammatical errors and given that this typed letter purports to be written by a medical doctor practicing in Toronto, it does not make sense why it contains such errors. For instance, the letter provides that the claimant "is suffering sever anxiety" <emphasis added>; the claimant "gets flashback"; the claimant "is much frittered from the thought"; "the same person who kidnapped her still looking for her"; the claimant "has been stocked and threatened a harassed"; the panel counted at least three instances in the short two-paragraph letter where there is a comma when there should be a period.
[22] The panel finds that, while the psychologist (of in this case a doctor or counsellor) may find evidence of anxiety and post-traumatic stress, this is not persuasive evidence for the panel that the person is in any position to state categorically that the claimant experienced what she informed the panel that she did. The panel notes that these documents do not establish satisfactorily or persuasively that the claimant's conditions are the result of what she is alleging in respect of her refugee claim. As stated in Danailov, with respect to the assessment of a physician's evidence and the question of the assessment of credibility, "that opinion evidence is only as valid as the truth of the facts on which it is based".4
[23] The panel has found the claimant's allegations to lack credibility. The panel gives these letters no weight in terms of establishing the claimant's allegations. As stated in Rokni, a psychiatric report (or in this case a letter from a doctor or counsellor) submitted as evidence "cannot possibly serve as a cure-all for any and all deficiencies in a claimant's testimony".5
DETERMINATION
[24] The panel finds that the claimant is not a Convention refugee as she does not have a well-founded fear of persecution for a Convention ground in Georgia. The panel also finds that the claimant is not a person in need of protection in that her removal to Georgia would not subject her personally to a risk to her life or to a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment, and in that there are no substantial grounds to believe that her removal to Georgia will subject her personally to a danger of torture.
[25] The panel also finds that the male and minor claimants are not Convention refugee as they do not have a well-founded fear of persecution for a Convention ground in Greece. The panel also finds that they are not persons in need of protection in that their removal to Greece would not subject them personally to a risk to their lives or to a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment, and in that there are no substantial grounds to believe that their removals to Greece will subject them personally to a danger of torture.
ANALYSIS
Identity
[26] The claimant established her personal identity and her identity as a national of Georgia through her testimony and the documents she presented.6
[27] The male and minor claimants established their personal identities and their identities as nationals of Greece through the male claimant's testimony and the documents they presented.7
Credibility
[28] On the basis of the cumulative credibility problems noted below, the panel finds, on a balance of probabilities, that the claimants are not being pursued by XXXX and his acquaintances, as they allege. The panel further finds, on a balance of probabilities, that the male claimant is not being pursued by the mafia as he alleges.
The Claimant is Held for Two Days
[29] The claimant says that XXXX and his friends kidnapped her in XXXX 2004. She says she was kept in a house for two days. The panel asked her whether she knows whose house this was, to which she replied in the negative. However, as the panel noted, the claimant stated in her PIF narrative that she was kept at XXXX friend's house. In response, the claimant stated that she does not know for sure, but this is her understanding. The panel does not accept her explanation. It is not for the panel to sift through the claimant's evidence, wondering what is based on speculation and what is not. The claimant's PIF narrative provides, as a matter of fact, that she was kept at XXXX friend's house. Her inconsistent evidence in this regard detracts from her credibility.
XXXX is Related to XXXX
[30] The claimant stated that she was kidnapped by XXXX and his friends in XXXX 2004. When testifying as to what she was doing at the time this incident took place, she stated that she was traveling in a taxi with XXXX when XXXX asked to stop the taxi in order to pick up something from the nearby store. When the taxi stopped and XXXX went inside the store, three men entered the taxi and the taxi drove off with the claimant. The panel asked the claimant what the taxi driver did at the time these men entered the vehicle. She said, "Nothing" and went on to say that it was all arranged because XXXX was related to XXXX. The panel noted that the claimant made no mention of XXXX involvement with and/or relation to XXXX in her PIF narrative or any of her subsequent multiple amendments. She was given the chance to explain why. She replied that the paralegal who assisted her in completing her first PIF did not put down her story correctly. For the following reasons, the panel does not accept her explanation, and her omission in this regard detracts from her credibility.
[31] The claimant signed that PIF as being complete, true and correct and while she made subsequent amendments to her PIF narrative, including additions and changes to what was originally stated, she did not include this part of her story as an amendment. The claimant repeatedly amended her PIF narrative up until the point of the hearing. The panel asked her why she made such amendments, to which she replied that she wanted to give the truth. The claimant studied XXXX at university in Georgia. She is not unsophisticated or uneducated. She has 14 years of formal education. It was open to her to amend her PIF narrative and she was fully aware of this. Indeed, the claimant amended her PIF narrative in many ways without including XXXX relationship to XXXX and her involvement in her kidnapping. It was open to her to amend her PIF accordingly. Instead, she went on to declare at the start of the hearing that the content of her PIF was complete, true and correct.
The Claimant seeks Medical Attention
[32] The panel asked the claimant whether she ever sought medical attention in Georgia. She replied, "No". When asked why, she stated that she always wanted to conceal things. However, when the claimant first made her refugee claim and spoke with an Immigration Officer (IO), her Record of Examination (RoE)8 provides that she received medical attention in Georgia. When the panel noted this to the claimant, she stated that she does not know what is written but she was medically examined because her body was badly bruised. The panel does not accept her explanation because it results in a shifting of her evidence. Either she sought medical attention or she did not. It is not for the panel to remind the claimant of her own evidence. Her inconsistent evidence in this regard detracts from her credibility.
XXXX Seeks Medical Attention
[33] The claimant testified that she sought medical attention after she was injured when XXXX tried to kidnap her in XXXX 2003. The panel noted that the claimant makes no mention of seeking medical attention in her PIF narrative or amendments and was given the opportunity to explain why. She stated that she explained her situation to her paralegal so she does not know whether she put it down. The panel does not accept her explanation.
[34] The claimant signed that PIF as being complete, true and correct and while she made subsequent amendments to her PIF narrative, including additions and changes to what was originally stated, she did not include this part of her story as an amendment. The claimant repeatedly amended her PIF narrative up until the point of the hearing because, as she explained, she wanted to give the truth. Just as she amended her PIF in other ways, it was open to her to amend her PIF narrative in this respect. Moreover, the panel notes that the PIF narrative (Question 31) directs the claimant to "state whether you have received any medical or psychological treatment or assessments in Canada or elsewhere relating to your claim." Her evidence as noted detracts from her credibility.
The Medical Report9
[35] The claimant says that she gave the police a medical report she obtained after the attempted kidnapping attempt in XXXX 2003. The panel asked her where this medical report that she gave the police is. She stated that the police kept it, adding that when her father went to the police and requested it back, they said the case is too old and did not have it on record. The panel asked her whether she made any effort, such as contacting the hospital or clinic, to obtain a copy to disclose to the Board. She replied that the hospital could not retrieve it from the archives despite her father's efforts.
[36] However, the panel subsequently noted that the claimant had, in fact, disclosed this medical document to the Board. The claimant was given the opportunity to clarify her evidence. She stated that her father wanted to obtain one directly from the hospital, not the police. The panel does not accept her explanation. Firstly, not only had the claimant stated that her father was unable to get such a document either from the police or the hospital, but she also explained why he she was unable to. It is not for the panel to remind the claimant of her own evidence. The panel recognises that, in her circumstances, the claimant might have difficulty remembering even important parts of her story. It is quite understandable if a claimant's memory fails her in some instances. What the panel has more difficulty in understanding is how the claimant can "remember" things which did not happen, such as that her father could not obtain this medical document and why. In this case, not only did the claimant fail to recall the very evidence she disclosed in support of her refugee claim, but she provided contradictory evidence with respect to such document. This detracts from her credibility.
[37] The panel further notes that this medical document does not name XXXX but a certain XXXX "and three other people" as the culprits. According to a PIF amendment, the claimant also including XXXX as being involved in this incident; however, so was XXXX and the fact that this document does not name XXXX - the very person the claimant is making a refugee claim against - does not make sense.
[38] Moreover, this document contains no security features. Also in light of all the credibility problems noted, the panel gives this document no weight in terms of establishing the claimant's allegations.
The Attempted Kidnapping
[39] The claimant says that in XXXX 2003, XXXX and his friends tried to pull her into the car and kidnap her. She says family members and neighbours were present at the time. The panel asked her whether anyone called the police, to which she replied, "No". However, as the panel noted, the claimant stated in her PIF narrative that the neighbours called the police on that occasion. She replied that she did not write that. Counsel submitted that the claimant wrote this based on hearsay. The panel accepts neither explanation. Firstly, the claimant signed that PIF as being interpreted to her and being complete, true and correct. This part of her PIF was not removed or changed in any of her amendments and it was open to her to do so. Moreover, it is not for the panel to sift through the claimant's evidence wondering if something is based on hearsay. The claimant's inconsistent evidence in this regard detracts from her credibility.
The Claimant's Father Seeks Police Help
[40] The claimant says that her father sought police help on her behalf. The claimant testified that when her father sought police help the first time, the police told him that there was no such case and, in fact, XXXX name was not included in the file. The panel asked the claimant whether the police said or did anything else, to which she replied, "No, nothing."
[41] The panel asked the claimant what the police said or did when he sought help the next time. She said that the police told him to go to another police office and when he went there the police said they had information that the claimant went to XXXX to meet him. When the panel asked her if the police said or did anything else, she replied in the negative saying, "Nothing".
[42] However, as the panel noted, in the claimant's first narrative, she stated that the police told her father to leave and not complain about XXXX because XXXX XXXX has a high position in the police and they were going to make it look as if the claimant ran away with him. The claimant replied that the paralegal who assisted her with her PIF took notes and wrote it. The panel does not accept her explanation. She signed that PIF as being complete, true and correct and while she made subsequent amendments to her PIF narrative, including additions and changes to what was originally stated, she did not amend her narrative in this respect. It was open to her to do this. Her inconsistent evidence in this regard detracts from her credibility.
The Police's Reaction
[43] The claimant says that despite her and her family's effort to seek police assistance, the police did not help her. The panel asked her if she knows why the police did not help them. She stated that, according to what her father told her, it was because XXXX paid a bribe to the police. She also stated that XXXX XXXX is in the police. In order to clarify her evidence, the panel asked the claimant whether it is because of the bribe or his XXXX being a police officer. She stated that XXXX bribed the police. The panel noted that the claimant did not say in her PIF narrative that XXXX bribed the police. She was given the opportunity to explain why. She stated that she did not know, adding that she was told that she could not add anything. The panel does not accept her explanation. The claimant amended her PIF narrative about three times prior to the hearing, which included adding information. Even if the claimant were told that she could not add anything, it is clear that she did not follow these instructions. Her allegation that the police did not help her because they were bribed is relevant to her claim. Certainly, the inability and/or unwillingness of the police to protect a claimant is material to a refugee claim. Her omission in this regard detracts from her credibility.
XXXX Telephones her Home
[44] The panel asked the claimant whether XXXX ever telephoned her home in Georgia. She replied in the negative. However, the claimant's PIF narrative provides that XXXX was "calling my home and threatening my parents". When the panel noted this to the claimant, she stated that she never said that. The panel does not accept her explanation. She declared in writing and at the start of the hearing that the content of her PIF was interpreted to her and was complete, true and correct. She signed that PIF as being complete, true and correct and while she made subsequent amendments to her PIF narrative, including additions and changes to what was originally stated, she did not amend her narrative to remove this part of her story. It was open to her to do this. Her inconsistent evidence in this regard detracts from her credibility.
When was the Claimant Assaulted
[45] The claimant says that XXXX sexually assaulted her in XXXX 2004. She testified that she informed her psychologist about this incident. The panel noted that the doctor's letter10 she disclosed provides that the claimant was kidnapped and sexually assaulted in XXXX 2003. Not only was the claimant not kidnapped in XXXX 2003, but she was not raped at that time either and the doctor does not provide for the incident of sexual assault in XXXX 2004. When the panel noted the inconsistent evidence, the claimant stated that she was beaten when XXXX first tried to kidnap her and she was sexually assaulted when she was kidnapped. The panel does not accept her explanation because it does not address the inconsistency. It does not make sense why this document would provide something else.
The Reason why the Claimant Failed to Seek Protection in Greece
[46] The claimant did not seek protection in Greece. She provided the panel with different reasons as to why she failed to seek protection in Greece:
[47] (i) When she first made a refugee claim, she informed the Immigration Officer (IO), as stated in her Record of Examination (RoE),11 that she did not seek protection in Greece because she did not speak Greek and she had no proof.
[48] (ii) On the other hand, her PIF provides that she did not seek protection in Greece because she thought she could not go without her husband.
[49] (iii) Finally, her oral evidence provided that she did not trust the police in Greece.
[50] The panel noted the different reasons she provided, giving her the chance to clarify her evidence. She stated that it was because of all three reasons and that her husband was beaten by the police and her brother was mistreated by them too. While it maybe that the claimant had three or more reasons for not seeking protection in Greece, she only referred to one when specifically asked by the panel in her hearing about her failure in doing so. Likewise, she only referred to one when she drafted her PIF. Again, she only referred to one in her RoE. If there are three reasons, then it is reasonable to expect her to have stated so to the panel when specifically asked about this and not only subsequent to the panel noting the claimant's own evidence to her.
[51] Furthermore, it was only in the hearing that the claimant informed the panel that her husband was beaten by the police and her brother was mistreated by them. When the panel asked her why this is not in her PIF narrative, she said it was the fault of her previous paralegal. The panel does not accept her explanation. She signed that PIF as being complete, true and correct and while she made subsequent amendments to her PIF narrative, including additions and changes to what was originally stated, she did not include this part of her story as an amendment. It was open to her to do this. Her evidence in this regard detracts from her credibility.
The Male Claimant's Contacts with the Mafia
[52] The male claimant says that the mafia has been pursuing him since XXXX 2007. The panel asked him when he first had any exchange with them, to which he replied it was on XXXX XXXX 2008. The panel asked him whether he had any problems before that time. He stated that he also found out that his employers were involved in illegal activity involving human trafficking.
[53] The panel asked the male claimant if he had any idea why the mafia had no exchange with him for 15 months after they started pursuing him. He then went on to testify how they kidnapped him on one occasion during these 15 months and held him for over an hour and a half, taking him to the seaside. The panel noted that the male claimant did not mention this incident in his PIF narrative or amendments and was given the opportunity to explain why. He stated that the mafia talked to him and he does not know exactly for how long they spoke to him. The panel does not accept his explanation because it does not address the omission. Moreover, it results in an inconsistency because the male claimant's initial testimony provided that his first exchange with the mafia was on XXXX XXXX 2008, and not before when he was kidnapped. Not only has the male claimant provided inconsistent evidence as to when he first had any contact with these people, but he failed to provide for such an incident in his written evidence. It is reasonable to expect his evidence with respect to his contacts with the very people he fears to be more consistent. His evidence in this regard undermines his credibility.
The Male Claimant's Efforts to Seek Police Help
[54] The panel asked the male claimant whether he ever sought police help in Greece. He replied in the affirmative. When the panel went on to question the male claimant about the number of times he sought police help, he then testified that he never sought police help in Greece. The panel asked him about his inconsistent evidence, giving him a chance to explain. He stated that he thought he was being asked if he saw the police helping people. The panel does not accept his explanation. The male claimant says that he cannot get protection in Greece because he does not trust the police and the police will not help him. At the same time, he says he saw the police helping people. Moreover, it does not make sense how the male claimant could have understood the question in such a way and there was nothing to indicate that he misunderstood until after the discrepancy was pointed out. There was no objection raised at any time in the hearing as to the quality of the interpretation. The male claimant's inconsistent evidence in this regard detracts from her credibility.
The Male Claimant Relocates to Avoid Being Caught
[55] The male claimant testified that he relocated from place to place to avoid being caught by the mafia. The panel noted that the claimant indicates in his PIF (Question 11) that he lived at the same address in Athens from XXXX XXXX 2007 until he came to Canada. In response, the male claimant stated that he went to a different address in Athens and he only moved once. The panel does not accept his explanation. Whether the male claimant relocated once or more than once, the fact is his PIF does not provide for this. Once the male claimant had to relocate in order to avoid harm, it is reasonable for the information contained in his PIF to be consistent in this regard. His inconsistent evidence detracts from his credibility.
The Male Claimant Misleads the Canadian Authorities when asking for Refugee Protection
[56] When the male claimant first made a refugee claim, he informed the IO, as provided in his RoE12 that he was a citizen of Georgia. He also spoke extensively as to the reason why he is making a refugee claim, which is based on his fear of someone in Georgia. As the notes from Citizenship and Immigration Canada (CIC)13 provide, they had information that the male claimant with the fact that he entered Canada with a Greek, not Georgian, passport. The male claimant subsequently changed his story, saying that he is a Greek citizen and provided a different reason as to why he is making a refugee claim, which is also based on his fear of returning to Greece. The panel asked the male claimant why he misled the Canadian authorities. He stated that he was afraid of being sent back to Greece. The panel does not accept his explanation. The very reason a person makes a refugee claim is not to be sent back to the very country where he fears returning.
[57] The panel recognises that refugees often have to resort to deception in order to leave the country of feared persecution and to enter a safe haven, such as Canada. Of greater concern to the panel is that the male claimant continued the misrepresentation after his arrival in Canada.
[58] In these circumstances, it is difficult to separate the male claimant's history of misrepresentation from his refugee claim. A misrepresentation in order to gain entry to Canada is significantly different from a lie to Canadian immigration officials or on a Personal Information Form, which a claimant declares to be true. To put it simply, if the male claimant was willing to engage in a serious misrepresentation to gain entry to Canada and to remain in Canada, how can the panel know that he is not engaging in other misrepresentations in order to succeed in his refugee claim? A refugee claimant is expected to pursue his claim diligently. The male claimant, in such circumstances, clearly did not.
[59] A claim may be rejected as lacking in credibility if the claimant's testimony is found to be incoherent or vague,14 or lacking in sufficient knowledge or detail reasonably expected of a person in the claimant's situation. The male claimant was evasive at times when testifying. For instance, when asked whether he has ever been arrested, he stated that he has not been arrested for something he did wrong. In order to clarify his evidence, the panel asked him to clarify whether he has ever been arrested. In response, he asked, "What do you mean?" It was clear from the claimant's first response (i.e. not for something he did wrong) that he understood what was being asked of him. The panel asked whether he has ever been arrested, imprisoned or detained. He replied that he has never been detained, without saying whether he has been arrested. The male claimant was given ample opportunity to testify as to whether he has ever been arrested.
[60] The male claimant finally went on to testify that he had been arrested several times by the Greek authorities and when asked what he was arrested for, he said, "Nothing". The panel noted that the claimant replied, "No" in his PIF (Question 9) when asked if he had ever been arrested. In response, the male claimant stated that he must have misunderstood the question. The panel does not accept his explanation. He was assisted by counsel. He declared in writing and at the start of the hearing that the content of his PIF was complete, true and correct. Not only did the claimant mislead the Canadian authorities when he initially made his refugee claim with respect to his nationality and the very basis of his refugee claim, he again misled the Canadian authorities with respect to his past experiences of arrest. He also provided evasive evidence as to any criminal history, as above noted. He only changed his story when confronted with contradictory evidence. The male claimant's evidence in this regard detracts from his credibility.
Failure to Claim
[61] Notwithstanding her reasons for fleeing Georgia, the claimant did not seek refugee protection in Greece because, as she testified, the Greek authorities do not treat refugees and immigrants well and XXXX threatened her not to go to the police. The claimant's explanation is inconsistent with her actions: despite her alleged fear of XXXX and the Greek authorities, she continued living in Greece for about 18 months.
Delay in Claiming
[62] The male claimant arrived in Canada on XXXX XXXX 2008. Despite his reasons for fleeing Greece, the male claimant waited about seven months before seeking protection in Canada. When asked why he failed to seek protection earlier, he stated that he did not know of the refugee system. The panel does not accept his explanation. It is the claimant's evidence that the Greek authorities do not treat refugees well. The male claimant had experiences with immigration systems, having obtained Greek citizenship as an adult. When the panel asked him about any enquiries he made, he stated that he sought legal advice in Canada, but he cold not recall the names of the lawyers. Moreover, as noted above, when the male claimant finally made a refugee claim, he misrepresented himself by alleging to be from another country.
[63] Even without disbelieving every part of a claimant's testimony, a panel may find the claimant so lacking in credibility that it concludes there is no credible evidence relevant to his claim.15 A claimant's contradictory testimony can cast doubt upon the totality of his oral evidence.16 These claimants had difficulties in the key areas of their evidence as noted. The panel finds that the claimants are lacking in credibility generally and that this lack of credibility extends to all of their relevant testimony.
State Protection
[64] The male claimant says that if he did not have problems with the mafia he would return to Greece. The panel has found these allegations not to be credible. The panel has gone on to examine the issue of state protection in Greece.
[65] A state, unless in complete breakdown, is presumed to be capable of protecting its citizens; international protection comes into play only when a refugee claimant cannot obtain protection domestically. A claimant is required to approach the state for protection if protection might reasonably be forthcoming or, alternatively, if it is objectively reasonable for the claimant to have sought protection. 17
[66] A claimant who alleges that state protection is inadequate must persuade the Board, on a balance of probabilities, that the evidence establishes this inadequacy,18 and such evidence must be clear and convincing.19 The claimant's burden of proof is directly proportional to the level of democracy in the state in question: the more democratic the state's institutions, the more the claimant must have done to exhaust all available courses of action.20 A claimant from a democratic country will have a heavy burden when attempting to show that he should not have been required to exhaust all the recourses available domestically before claiming refugee status.21
[67] The adequacy of state protection cannot rest on the subjective fear of the claimant22; a claimant cannot rebut the presumption of state protection in a functioning democracy by asserting only a subjective reluctance to engage the state.23
[68] Greece is a constitutional republic and multiparty parliamentary democracy. The constitution and law provide citizens the right to change their government peacefully, and citizens exercised this right in practice through periodic, free, and fair elections based on universal suffrage.24
[69] According to the US Department of State report for 2011, the most important human rights problem during the year concerned the government's treatment of undocumented migrants. In 2011, Greece continued to face a humanitarian crisis in the detention of illegal immigrants as well as in its prison system. There were reports of human rights abuses in the cases of illegal immigrants and asylum seekers. In this case, the claimants are all documented and had status - the male claimant and daughter are, in fact, citizens. They form part of the ten percent of the population in Greece which consists of immigrants.25
[70] Other human rights problems cited during the year included alleged abuse of detainees by security forces, laws restricting freedom of speech, restrictions on religious freedom, deportation of unaccompanied immigrant minors, inadequate reception capacity or legal aid for asylum seekers and refugees, domestic violence, incidents of anti-Semitism, trafficking in persons, limits on the freedom of certain ethnic minority groups to self-identify, and discrimination against and social exclusion of ethnic minorities. There were also allegations of excessive use of force against protestors during violent demonstrations in which police officers were also injured.
[71] However, the government has taken steps to prosecute and punish officials who committed abuses, whether in the security services or elsewhere in the government.26
[72] There were no reports that the government or its agents committed arbitrary or unlawful killings in 2011. Also, there were no reports of politically motivated disappearances in 2011. The law prohibits torture and other cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment or punishment and provides safeguards for criminal suspects and undocumented immigrants in detention.27
[73] The law provides for an independent judiciary, and the government generally respected this provision in practice. However, observers reported the judiciary was inefficient and sometimes subject to influence and corruption. The law provides for the right to a fair trial, and an independent judiciary generally enforced this right. Defendants enjoy a presumption of innocence. There were no reports of political prisoners or detainees. On three occasions during 2011, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) found that the country had violated provisions of the European Convention on Human Rights with regard to the right to a fair trial within a reasonable period. The country paid the compensation ordered by the court.
[74] There is a generally independent and impartial judiciary in civil matters. The law provides citizens with the ability to sue the government for compensation for alleged violations of rights. Individuals may seek civil remedies for human rights violations and appeal decisions to the ECHR. Restitution is available. The government complied with domestic and ECHR decisions.
[75] There have also been steps against those responsible for human rights' abuses. In May 2011 the Hellenic National Police authority suspended three police officers from duty for their role in allegedly injuring a demonstrator during a violent protest in Athens. Some NGOs alleged that police used excessive force in efforts to control the violence; however, the police were not responsible for all of the injuries demonstrators sustained. On December 13, 2011, a police officer was sentenced to a six-year suspended sentence for the aggravated torturing with electrical shocks of two young men in August 2002. On December 19, 2011, the courts sentenced two police officers to suspended prison sentences of five years and five-and-a half years respectively for beating Afghans during a police raid in an Athens apartment building in December 2004.28
[76] In addressing the problem, Greek law provides for an independent ombudsman office to deal with reports of different abuses. The Office of the Ombudsman provided an effective means for citizens to report human rights problems. The office received adequate resources to perform its various functions, which included mediating between private individuals and public administrators and defending and promoting children's rights. There were five deputy ombudsmen who dealt, respectively, with human rights, children's rights, citizen-state relations, health and social welfare, and quality of life problems.29
[77] Furthermore, the government-funded National Commission for Human Rights (NCHR) is an autonomous human rights body that advises the government on the protection of human rights. It is composed of representatives of the government, labor unions, political parties, civil society organizations, independent authorities, and academics.30
[78] The law provides criminal penalties for official corruption. There are signs that the authorities are taking action in this area. In September 2010 the public prosecutor's office set up a new unit to facilitate prosecution of economic/financial crimes in the public sector. Authorities designed the unit to work closely with the financial and economic crime unit of the Ministry of Finance and the internal affairs division of the State Police, which was responsible for corruption cases across the public sector.31
[79] Reports of police corruption continued in 2011. During the year the police bureau of internal affairs took multiple disciplinary measures, including dismissal and suspension, against officers involved in corruption.32
[80] The Hellenic Police Internal Affairs Unit investigated allegations of corruption and police impunity. The 2010 report of the Internal Police Affairs Unit noted a 101 percent increase in corruption cases (from 389 to 785) since 2009 in the police and the public sector in general.33 There is evidence, therefore, that the Greek authorities are taking steps against corruption.
[81] There are income disclosure laws for high-ranking public officials and members of parliament. Such information was generally provided and made public. The law provides for the right of access to government-held information.34
[82] There is also evidence of the Greek authorities' efforts to prosecute the expression of racist ideas. In March 2010, human rights NGOs filed a protest letter with the Athens prosecutor regarding a coast guard unit (39 members) that chanted racist slogans during the Independence Day (March 25) parade in Athens that year. The prosecutor filed charges against unit members. In December two of the 39 defendants were convicted and each sentenced to a three and a half month suspended prison sentence.35
[83] There have been improvements over the last number of years. In 2011, the year police and coast guard personnel received training from government ministries, regional and international organizations, NGOs, and service academies. Subjects included antiracism, anti-trafficking and victim identification, asylum-seeker recognition, witness protection and interviewing, and crowd control.36
[84] The constitution provides for freedom of speech and press, and the government generally respected these rights in practice. Individuals could criticize the government publicly or privately without reprisal, and the government did not impede criticism. However, the law provides for prosecution of individuals who "intentionally incite others to actions that could provoke discrimination, hatred, or violence against persons or groups of persons on the basis of their race or ethnic origin or who express ideas insulting to persons or to groups of persons because of their race or ethnic origin." Independent media were active and expressed a wide variety of views. There were no government restrictions on access to the Internet or reports that the government monitored e-mail or Internet chat rooms.37
[85] There were no government restrictions on academic freedom or cultural events. There were no reports of interference with cultural events of various ethnic groups. The constitution and law provide for freedom of assembly, and the government generally respected this right in practice.
[86] A variety of domestic and international human rights groups generally operated without government restriction, investigating and publishing their findings on human rights cases. Government officials were often cooperative and responsive to their views.38
[87] There is evidence of incidents of mistreatment and discrimination against certain minorities. Yet, perfect protection is not the applicable standard, but rather whether the police make serious efforts to protect the citizens.39 Indeed, the male claimant's own evidence shows that the Greek authorities have taken action against corruption and illegality: he says that people involved in the illegal trafficking scheme had been arrested.
[88] The male claimant says that the police in Greece are unable to protect him because he fears a group of people and not one person. The panel notes that doubting the effectiveness of the protection offered by the state when one has not really tested it does not rebut the existence of a presumption of state protection.
[89] In light of the foregoing, the panel is not persuaded that there is clear and convincing evidence that the state would not be reasonably forthcoming with adequate protection to protect the male claimant from the persons whom he fears if he were to return to Greece.
[90] Based on the above analysis and after considering all of the evidence, the panel finds, on a balance of probabilities, that state protection is available to the male and minor claimants should they return to Greece.
CONCLUSION
[91] The panel has considered all three protection grounds under s. 96 and ss. 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.
[92] On the basis of the totality of all the evidence made available to the panel, finds that there is not a serious possibility that the claimant would be persecuted in Georgia, nor that she would be subjected personally to a risk to her life, cruel and unusual treatment or punishment, or to a danger of torture.
[93] On the basis of the totality of all the evidence made available to the panel, finds that there is not a serious possibility that the male and minor claimants would be persecuted in Greece, nor that they would be subjected personally to a risk to their lives, cruel and unusual treatment or punishment, or to a danger of torture.
[94] For these reasons, the claims are rejected.
(signed)
"C. Buttigieg"
C. Buttigieg
26 October 2012
